topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — verdict changed to Self-Contradicted via Refraction Cascade
change_summary: Verdict changed from 'Std Model Explains' to 'Self-Contradicted' (EXP-078 Refraction Cascade). detail_evidence +23%, detail_verdict_text +276%. TLDRs and finding field not updated to match new verdict.
our_argument_summary: The cascade argument: the dome's apparent/physical sun altitude split makes the physical sun position seasonally indeterminate (1,975–3,618 km). Eclipse shadow positions depend on physical sun altitude, so eclipse-based predictions inherit this indeterminacy. Combined with the existing argument that Chapman (1933) Sq current mechanism explains all observations. Verdict changed from 'Std Model Explains' to 'Self-Contradicted' because the dome's own refraction fix breaks eclipse geometry predictions.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring pipeline exists for WIN-068 specifically. The historical 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation (Kim & Chang 2018). The 2026 eclipse predictions (E001-E009) use the formula delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF, where FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry. This FSF explicitly uses H(r) — the same function that creates the refraction cascade. The dome's own code thus ties its eclipse predictions to the function whose indeterminacy we cite.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No validation exists for WIN-068 in monitor.py. Eclipse Domain 11 is a future prediction for Aug 2026, not a check on historical data.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang 2018 (literature citation, not API fetch)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse evidence at Tier 1 (non-discriminating). The 2026 eclipse has a Kp < 2 precondition that voids the test ~50% of the time.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — monitoring='none' is correct for WIN-068.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-068 cites Kim & Chang (2018) historical data. Eclipse Domain 11 in live power dashboard is for the 2026 future event. No API call, no comparison logic for the 39-eclipse ensemble. Tag: none.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Eclipse geomagnetic effects explained by Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq current mechanism for 93 years. The dome relabels this as 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading.' The dome's own PROS-003 description ('geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome') is the Chapman mechanism with vocabulary substitution.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) published their 39-eclipse analysis covering 1991–2016. The dome model incorporated this as a WIN in V51.1 (2026). All 39 eclipses predate the dome's claim by years to decades.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome asserts 'aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow' but provides no equation deriving expected magnitude, component pattern, latitude dependence, or timing from dome geometry for the historical ensemble. The 2026 predictions DO derive values from FSF/H(r), but the historical 39-eclipse ensemble claim is qualitative assertion without quantitative derivation.

holes_found.description: VERDICT-TEXT MISMATCH: The verdict is 'Self-Contradicted' but the detail_evidence, detail_verdict_text, finding, tldr_evidence, and tldr_verdict all describe a 'Std Model Explains' argument. The Refraction Cascade — the actual basis for the Self-Contradicted verdict — appears nowhere in WIN-068's own entry text. A reader who encounters WIN-068 in the summary table sees 'Self-Contradicted' but the expanded detail text explains standard physics and triple-counting. The self-contradiction argument exists only in Section 1.8's cascade table. This is the worst kind of hole: the verdict badge and the evidence disagree, and a dome defender will immediately call it out as a review error where we slapped a harsher verdict on without updating the justification.
holes_found.recommendation: The detail_evidence must include a paragraph explicitly stating the self-contradiction: 'Independent of the standard physics explanation, this WIN is internally contradicted by the dome's own refraction fix. Section 1.8 documents how splitting sun altitude into apparent (5,733 km) and physical (<4,200 km) makes the physical sun position seasonally indeterminate — varying from ~3,618 km at northern summer to ~1,975 km at southern summer. Eclipse shadow geometry requires a definite physical sun altitude. Across 39 eclipses spanning different seasons and geometries, the dome cannot specify where the physical sun was for any of them, making the shadow-tracking prediction indeterminate.' The finding field must mention self-contradiction. The TLDRs must be rewritten.

holes_found.description: CROSS-WIN INCONSISTENCY IN CASCADE APPLICATION: WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse) was changed to Self-Contradicted and appears in the Section 1.8 cascade table. WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble) was changed to Self-Contradicted and appears in the cascade table. But WIN-025 (2024 9-station eclipse confirmation) remains 'Std Model Explains' and is NOT listed in the Section 1.8 cascade table. All three are in the same detail_group (WIN-010/025/068) and all three cite eclipse magnetic effects that depend on eclipse shadow geometry. If the cascade breaks eclipse geometry predictions, it must break all three equally. A dome defender will ask: 'Why does the same cascade argument change WIN-010 and WIN-068 but not WIN-025?' This asymmetry undermines the cascade's credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Either add WIN-025 to the Section 1.8 cascade table and change its verdict to Self-Contradicted, or explain why WIN-025 is immune to the cascade (e.g., if WIN-025 is removed/not active). Check whether WIN-025 has any special status that exempts it.

holes_found.description: FINDING FIELD OMITS SELF-CONTRADICTION: The finding field reads 'Triple-counts WIN-010/WIN-025; Chapman Sq mechanism (1933) explains all; dome provides no magnitude derivation' — a summary appropriate for 'Std Model Explains' or 'Misleading.' For a Self-Contradicted verdict, the finding must mention the actual self-contradiction. This is the one-line summary that most readers see in the summary table. If it doesn't mention what's self-contradicted, readers will be confused by the verdict badge.
holes_found.recommendation: Revise finding to something like: 'Dome's sun-altitude refraction fix makes eclipse shadow geometry indeterminate (Section 1.8); also triple-counts WIN-010/025 and relabels Chapman (1933) ionospheric physics.'

holes_found.description: TLDR FIELDS DESCRIBE WRONG VERDICT: tldr_evidence says 'the dome cites a real paper but relabels a 93-year-old standard explanation' — this is 'Std Model Explains' language. tldr_verdict says 'The dome relabels Chapman's mechanism without adding new physics' — also 'Std Model Explains.' Neither TLDR mentions self-contradiction, the refraction cascade, or the sun altitude indeterminacy. Since TLDRs are what readers see BEFORE expanding details, this is a major mismatch that will immediately confuse readers.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite tldr_evidence to lead with the self-contradiction: 'The dome's own refraction fix makes the physical sun altitude indeterminate (varying 1,975–3,618 km by season), so it cannot specify eclipse shadow geometry across 39 eclipses. Additionally, eclipse geomagnetic effects have been explained by standard ionospheric physics since Chapman (1933).' Rewrite tldr_verdict similarly.

holes_found.description: The detail_verdict_text contains a generic post-hoc boilerplate paragraph ('Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test...') that dilutes the Self-Contradicted argument. The post-hoc test is a secondary concern; the primary argument should be the self-contradiction. For a Self-Contradicted verdict, the first paragraph of the verdict text should state the self-contradiction, with post-hoc as supplementary.
holes_found.recommendation: Restructure detail_verdict_text to lead with the refraction cascade self-contradiction argument, followed by the Chapman mechanism point, with the post-hoc boilerplate as a final supplementary paragraph.

holes_found.description: ADVOCATE MODE CONCERN: The dome defender's strongest counter to the cascade argument specifically for eclipses is: 'The eclipse magnetic prediction tracks observed shadow geometry (apparent positions), not physical positions. The Sq current mechanism depends on photoionization reduction, which follows the observed shadow path regardless of the sun's physical altitude. The refraction cascade doesn't break the prediction because the magnetically-relevant shadow is the one observers see.' This defense has force rating 3/5 because the Sq current reduction IS tied to the observed shadow, and the magnetic effect IS measured at ground level where apparent geometry determines shadow coverage. Our text needs to preempt this by explaining that the dome's own mechanism ('geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome') invokes a physical field interaction, not an apparent-position effect.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to the cascade argument in WIN-068's detail_evidence: 'The dome's claimed mechanism is a physical field interaction (aetheric field perturbation by shadow geometry), not an apparent-position effect. This requires the physical sun position, which the refraction fix makes indeterminate.'

holes_found.description: The C2 review flagged a wrong Chapman 1933 DOI. The current text avoids a DOI link entirely (citing Chapman 1933 by journal name only), which sidesteps the issue. However, the Yamazaki & Maute 2021 Frontiers link is now correctly attributed rather than labeled 'Ashour-Chapman model.' Both C2 citation issues appear addressed.
holes_found.recommendation: No action needed — C2 citation issues resolved.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's 2026 eclipse predictions (E001-E009) explicitly use FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry. This means the dome's OWN future predictions use H(r), which is the function that creates the cascade. The cascade doesn't just affect retroactive claims — it renders the dome's forward-looking quantitative predictions indeterminate. This could be cited in WIN-068's text to strengthen the self-contradiction argument: 'The dome's own 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF derives from H(r)) explicitly depends on the function whose seasonal indeterminacy Section 1.8 documents.'

The dome's three eclipse WINs use three different magnitude values (-10.9 nT single-station, ~-10 nT 9-station average, unstated for 39-eclipse ensemble) while the 2026 predictions have different baseline options (BOU baseline vs W004 baseline giving predictions ~2x different). This internal magnitude inconsistency is separate from the cascade but reinforces the 'Self-Contradicted' verdict. WIN-068 could note: 'The dome cannot even state a consistent expected magnetic magnitude — its predictions range from -5.0 to -26.2 nT across the 2026 stations depending on which baseline is used.'

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's ensemble approach is methodologically sound in principle. Aggregating data across 39 eclipses rather than relying on single events shows awareness of statistical rigor. Kim & Chang (2018) IS excellent peer-reviewed science. And the dome's willingness to register quantitative predictions for the 2026 eclipse (E001-E009 with specific station-by-station nT values) demonstrates genuine predictive commitment that most alternative models never attempt. The dome also correctly identifies that eclipses produce measurable geomagnetic effects.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The ensemble aggregation doesn't create new physics — it's the same ionospheric conductivity mechanism at higher statistical power. The dome's own refraction fix creates an internal contradiction that prevents it from specifying the eclipse geometry it claims to predict. And registering 2026 predictions using H(r)-derived FSF doubles down on the function whose indeterminacy the cascade exposes.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender reads our six methodological principles and argues: 'Principle 4 requires you to address the strongest version of the dome's claim. The eclipse magnetic effect TRACKS OBSERVED SHADOW GEOMETRY — the dome's prediction is that the effect follows the apparent shadow path, not that it depends on the sun's physical altitude. The Sq current mechanism works via photoionization reduction in the ionosphere, which follows the observed shadow coverage fraction, not the physical sun position. Your refraction cascade argument is irrelevant to this prediction because the magnetically-relevant quantity is the shadow coverage fraction at ionospheric altitude, which is determined by the sun-moon alignment as seen from below — i.e., the apparent geometry. The dome's mechanism may invoke "physical field interaction" in vague language, but the actual prediction (magnetic anomaly tracks eclipse geometry) is validated by apparent positions that are PRESERVED by the refraction fix. You changed the verdict based on an argument that doesn't apply to this specific measurement type.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add explicit text in the cascade argument explaining WHY physical (not apparent) sun position matters for eclipse magnetic predictions in the dome model. The key: the dome's claimed mechanism is 'aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow BLOCKING THE FIELD INTERACTION BETWEEN SUN AND DOME.' If the mechanism is a physical field being physically blocked, the blocking depends on physical positions, not apparent ones. A photoionization effect (standard physics) tracks apparent shadow; a physical field blockage (dome physics) tracks physical positions. This distinction makes the cascade argument model-dependent in the right way — it specifically attacks the dome's mechanism while acknowledging the standard mechanism is unaffected.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-010 is Self-Contradicted (cascade), WIN-068 is Self-Contradicted (cascade), but WIN-025 (same detail_group, same phenomenon, 2024 9-station eclipse confirmation) remains 'Std Model Explains' and is absent from the Section 1.8 cascade table. All three cite eclipse magnetic effects depending on eclipse shadow geometry. If the cascade breaks eclipse geometry for WIN-010 and WIN-068, it must break WIN-025 equally. This asymmetry is the most immediately exploitable inconsistency in our review — a dome defender will point out we selectively applied the cascade. Additionally, WIN-012 (coupling constant κ = 1.67 nT/μGal) depends on the BOU 2017 eclipse magnitude (-10.9 nT). If that magnitude's prediction is self-contradicted, the coupling constant derivation is also compromised, yet WIN-012 remains 'Self-Contradicted' for a different reason (κ denominator vanishing). The cascade arguably reinforces WIN-012's existing verdict but through a separate pathway that isn't documented.

claims_checked: H(r) at Tropic of Cancer (r ≈ 7,400 km): 8537 × exp(-7400/8619) = 3,618 km ✓ (Section 1.8 says ~3,618 km)
H(r) at Tropic of Capricorn (r ≈ 12,600 km): 8537 × exp(-12600/8619) = 1,979 km ✓ (Section 1.8 says ~1,975 km)
5,733 km > 3,618 km (sun altitude exceeds firmament at Cancer) ✓ — collision confirmed
93 years between Chapman 1933 and dome V51.1 2026 ✓
Kim & Chang metadata: 39 eclipses, 100 stations, 207 manifestations, 1991-2016 ✓
quantitative_verification.computations: Verified the firmament height function H(r) = 8537 × exp(-r/8619) at key orbital radii using Python. All Section 1.8 cascade calculations match. Verified the sun altitude collision at both tropical radii.

deeper_analysis:
The Self-Contradicted verdict is substantively correct — the refraction cascade logic is mathematically sound and the dome's eclipse predictions DO depend on H(r) through the FSF factor. However, the transition from 'Std Model Explains' to 'Self-Contradicted' was incompletely executed: the verdict was changed but the supporting text was not rewritten. This is the most actionable finding.

The critical text-verdict mismatch means that right now, WIN-068 reads like a 'Std Model Explains' entry wearing a 'Self-Contradicted' badge. A dome defender who reads only the detail text will correctly observe that our evidence argues standard physics explains the phenomenon — not that the dome contradicts itself. The self-contradiction argument exists only in Section 1.8, which is a cross-cutting analysis section. The WIN entry itself should contain a self-standing self-contradiction argument, with a link to Section 1.8 for the full cascade analysis.

The cross-WIN inconsistency with WIN-025 is the second most serious finding. It's a straightforward gap: the cascade was applied to WIN-010 and WIN-068 but not WIN-025, despite all three being in the same detail_group. This must be resolved to maintain credibility.

The advocate mode defense (apparent vs physical shadow geometry) is the most intellectually interesting challenge. It has genuine force: ionospheric photoionization reduction tracks the observed shadow, not the physical sun position. Our preemptive response must distinguish between the standard mechanism (apparent shadow) and the dome's claimed mechanism (physical field blockage). This distinction is the crux — if we can establish that the dome's mechanism requires physical positions, the cascade holds; if the dome can retreat to 'the effect tracks apparent positions,' the cascade doesn't bite here.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) — correct journal, volume, pages, content description
Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3) — correct venue (was previously wrong DOI, now cited by journal name only)
Yamazaki & Maute 2021 (Frontiers link https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fspas.2021.766327/full) — correctly attributed to review authors rather than 'Ashour-Chapman model'
citations_unchecked: The Part 3.1b post-hoc standard link (#posthoc-standard) — internal cross-reference, assumed valid
